reason: The text emphasizes and reinforces hierarchy at multiple levels throughout the narrative. God commands Moses; Moses leads and instructs the Israelites (e.g., 'אַל־ תִּירָאוּ' - commanding them not to fear); Pharaoh exercises supreme authority over Egypt and commands his servants and military forces (with officers 'שָׁלִשִׁים' mentioned). The entire account of the Exodus is structured around hierarchical relationships: divine authority (YHWH) at the top, followed by human leaders (Moses for Israel, Pharaoh for Egypt) exercising authority over their respective populations. The narrative presupposes and reinforces clear chains of command and authority rather than discouraging or minimizing hierarchical organization.
